item.title: Object Class
item.content:encoded:
I feel that SCP-1152 should not be classified as Safe, especially since it has repeatedly broken containment. Under the ACS system, I think it should be:

Clearance Level
Unrestricted
Containment Class
Euclid
Disruption Class
Dark
Risk Class
Notice

item.title: Re: Staff Post- Licensing, Open
item.content:encoded: Ah, thank you very much for responding so quickly! :D

item.title: Staff Post- Licensing, Open
item.content:encoded:
Thanks to C-Dives, an alternative image has been found for this article, the source of which is compliant with our site's license. Image is here and is CC BY SA 2.0.

This post is being made to inform the author and userbase that I have changed the image with ProcyonLotor's permission.

E: i r englesh gud

item.title: Admin Post - Info - Open
item.content:encoded: Wraith1212 , instead of replying to your own comments, please edit your previous post using the "edit" function under the "options" tab to the lower right of every comment. That prevents spam buildup, and it's in the rules.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: That being said I really like berserks ideas too

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I like it a lot, it takes itself seriously enough not to be a joke article but is silly and short enough to be really entertaining. I think the idea that the foundation presumes its randomly pressing buttons is in line with the foundation dealing with a cognitohazard it isn't aware of yet, it seems out of character for them, but also so is seeing a racoon where a man pretty definately is

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: It is a good article when things like RASA and stuff were not created and accepted by various authors in 2014. It is a lore difference. I think in the lore right now the foundation is way much more powerful than it used to be, since reality bender and cognition influence etc are no longer "untreatable" and foundation actually has a counter to everything. I mean come on we are letting foundation resetting the world rn. Just take this article as "unupdated" in the history of foundation.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Well… It's a raccoon… But, how has it conserved that mass if it's undernourished?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I know this is old, but please foudnation- be smartest than this?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Thanks Zyn. I should've collapzed it in the original post.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
It is actually a pretty creepy read and it made me feel extremely bad for the agent.

But I would give the idea of doing a rewrite/expansion in the future to cover some roles in the whole article.

+ show block
– hide block

The Foundation has recurring characters that have certain awareness of cognito hazards (even on a reality altering level like the original stuff related to Dr Clef) and occurrences of cognito hazard are not so uncommon that they can not be perceived as cognito hazards, with The Foundation being able avoid them consciously. Like, we have entire documents logging the entire effects of the cognito hazard in question (Like the SCP that can change the perception of people's age, even to negative amounts. They still know it is a cognito hazard even though they never stop treating them as the perceived age)

To me the best way to move this article forward is to change from simple cognitohazard to actual reality bending cognito hazard (as in, even stuff he interacts with is affected, thus being unable to dial it correctly).

From the current way the article is written, the intelligence test is the main problem (Considering the part of the log specifically talking about how he has average post pubescent human intelligence). Any animal showing what can be described as a fully developed human level of cognizance, no matter what cognition hazard you put on him, he is still as intelligent as a grown adult human. It would instantly elevate him from "Simple Racoon with anomalous properties" to "Anomalous Racoon that has human intelligence and thinks he is Agent __".

Foundation personel is trained to find those inconsistencies, so 6 years is too much time for them not to be unto something. And again, this is not about "they should solve the problem". For all intents and purposes he would be still be treated as an SCP, like all strange sentient objects and animals they have in their possession. But they should have either never tested for intelligence for the current article not to feel like it would be more in line with the FBI guys that call the Foundation from time to time for help, or the kind of anomaly should have stronger effects, like the SCP that can't be remembered or retroactively described even if directly percieved or the one that the cognito hazard is so strong that they can't percieve any and all human contact through any means.

A stronger effect would either make them not even test for intelligence for some reason or mess with the test itself, removing any possible inconsistency with remaining foundation records.

It is specifically about the intelligence part. A human level intelligent raccoon would immediately move him from simple animal cage to actual figure of interest. Most intelligent SCPs, including those incapable of human like communication, are constantly being tested, psychologically evaluated and questioned. The moment the words "intelligence of an average post-pubescent human" entered the log it would immediately point to something fishy.

And again, Cognito Hazards are not uncommon, cataloging the awareness of something being a cognito hazard doesn't mean it stops being a cognito hazard and the Foundation stops treating it as the intended effect of the distortion. But the Foundation would never be unaware that something is a cognito hazard for 6 years. It is just too commonplace at this point.


As a courtesy to our readers on mobile devices, please collapse long posts. ~Zyn

So change the text so that the effect is stronger than just being perceived as a raccoon (thus negating it's Intelligence Score) or just change it so that the foundation is aware that it is a cognito hazard. It both being weak enough to allow that to pass and the Foundation not catching it seem kinda of negligent on their part.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This article is really good. That is probably one of the best plot twists in the wiki, specially because you have to discover it by yourself, the article does not outright tell you, it only leaves hints. I love this.
+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This is great, would like to see more photos and incident reports to expand on the nightmare this guy is living. For example photo of scratches in walls (in reality pleas for help written by the guy) etc

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Grrreugh, this gives the perfectly wrong kind of goosebumps. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Okay, my first thought is "ah, so that's what's going on", my second is "poor S.O.B", and my third is wondering if, given that the AICs have proven to be resistant or outright immune to mind affecting stuff in the past, they would be able to see him for what he really is if one of them ever got a look at him for whatever reason. Unfortunately for the Agent though it's unlikely to ever happen for multiple reasons but I wondered about it nevertheless.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Oh my. Part when he was trying to speak with his wife hit me into heart. But the most creepy shit is that he is fed by racoon food.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Because 99.87% and 100% is a huge difference when it comes to DNA stuff. We share 99% of our DNA with chimpanzees.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I "hate" this one.
This one confused me and I had to read the comments to get it.
A somewhat reluctant +1

item.title: Happy +100
item.content:encoded: This upvote was well earned. I am going to go look for a tale (pun intended) that might tell just who this raccoon pissed off to get thus cursed. Creatively done.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: You're looking at it backwards, really- the fridge horror comes at the point where it stops being funny. It is meant to stop being funny. This is the former Agent's reality, and no one will ever see him as anything but an oddly behaving racoon who is in no way the former Agent, for as long as he lives. He can plead, and they will hear chirping. He can write, and they will see only scratches. He can scream, and they will hear only screeches. There's a only a vaguely defined line between comedy and horror.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I… I'm sorry, but I just can't see why people like this. This is an excellent article, but in my opinion it would be more at home in the -J section than the mainstream SCP articles. I understand the anomaly, and understand what effect it has, but this article has more alike with some of the -J articles I've read than any mainstream article.

In comparison to another SCP that modifies its own description, such as the toaster that makes everything about it referred to in the first person, this has the same effect of "wait, what?" but the humor quickly drops off and it quickly became uninteresting. Once again, if this was a -J article, I would be upvoting it as much as I could because it carries the tone of a mainstream article while having underlying ridiculousness.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
So… Why the foundation view the agent as a raccoon? Is it an illusion or is it that the agent underwent some transformation?

Oh well, upvote for this.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Necroposting probably but:

SCP-1152 does not share the genome of other members of its species, but is a near complete match for the human genome. After logging SCP-1152's genome in the Foundation Database it was found to share 99.87% of its genetic code with the late Agent ████.1 Along with its human DNA, SCP-1152 has proven susceptible to pathogens normally restricted to infecting humans. SCP-1152 also has been verified to have a lifespan much longer than others of its species, having been in containment for six years.

This is why they're classifying it. As for how they tested it in the first place, presumably they saw the raccoon as doing something weird, and took it in for testing.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Seriously smug, I hadn't got it until after reading the comments. Superbly done altogether.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The concept is nice, but it didn't really grab me — I guess I don't like it for some reason. -1.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Et tu, Anborough?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Procyon lotor (common raccoon)

Oh shit is that what that phrase means.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Any such animal introduced to SCP-1152, even members of its own species, has displayed hostility to SCP-1152. As such it is now contained in a secluded habitat.

It's an SCP. It must be "contained in a secluded habitat".

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Huh. If it's viewed by all as an ordinary raccoon. Why classify it all? The fact that it is heavy and has human blood? Me-thinks the foundation would toss it into a incinerator once they got bored with having a heavy and human blooded raccoon to feed in the same shift as [[DATA REDACTED]] and [[DATA EXPUNGED]].

Guess these just aren't my cup of tea.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: just another pic of a raccoon in a cage

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Brilliant. I love how the true anomaly is completely unstated and entirely meta.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The miror is 1207 it started out as cognitohazrd that was effecting the foundation but not the reader but was eventually changed. It was also a case of a picture that was very obviously not what the description said it was.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: What do you mean "goes the way the mirror did"? (I'm just curious. It seems to be a reference I don't understand.)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Personally I prefer it this way, we the readers are immune to the cognitohazard because its fictional so we see the reality of the situation.

I will be really unhappy if this goes the way the miror did.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: It just seems weird that they would assume dialing that number was completely unintentional. Perhaps it's simply the way it's worded.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Again, this piece? dope.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Where did you find that picture? What's the point of taking that kind of picture?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
In my headcanon (I'm the author of 1539), semantic dissociation is just something that something happens — to the toaster, to 1539, and to this poor guy. It's a different sort of fridge horror from an identical scenario, and this is well written. (How many Evil Elder Things do we have?)

I don't see that as a downside. +1.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: As spoiler'd above it's not about not noticing. Even in the picture they can only perceive him as a racoon.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I think thats the anomally- no amount of evidence, visual or otherwise, will convince anyone having had contact with him that he is not a raccoon. He could steal a pen and paper and write " I am Agent ████" and a thorough explanation of what happened to him and all they'd see would be random marks. Its like unbreakable suspension of disbelief for the fact that he is a raccoon.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
The part in the addendum where the writer seems to figure that the SCP was just randomly pressing buttons and just happened to dial the wife of the man it shares DNA with sort of broke my suspension of disbelief. I understand that the Foundation would be hesitant to make certain conclusions regarding the SCP, perhaps even if they knew, but the amount of evidence presented in the article without comment just makes the Foundation look kind of dumb, at least to me. No vote, but this was an interesting read, and I did enjoy it mostly.

Edit: I've warmed up to this enough to upvote.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Reminded me a lot of SCP-247 and SCP-1539, but was unique enough to warrant an upvote. I can only imagine what horrendous events had to transpire to cause this guy to be perceived as a raccoon.
Fun Fact: I had no idea that Procyon Lotor was the Latin name for raccoon and assumed that Procyon's name was just gibberish. Derp.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: EDIT: fuck you 18yo me this article is dope

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Pretty simple, but still cool. Strikes me as a cool combo of mister lonely and the identity thieving hat.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Pretty good. +1. Well done, Vorcha.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: It seems strange that the Foundation would overlook the obvious here, but I guess you can chalk it up as part of the anomaly. My only real qualm with this is the similarity to SCP-1539, which Rio has already pointed out. Cepceptual similarities aside, I think this manages to set itself apart and is interesting enough to deserve a spot on the list. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I was slightly confused at first, but then suddenly came to realise its amazingness! +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
It's a very interesting and thought-provoking article. And upvote.
+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
It's basically a retread of the semantic dissociation zone, at least in terms of concept if not in storyline, and the entire article could essentially be condensed down to the picture, the caption, and the last log without having any real effect on how I viewed the article.

That said, it's a damn good picture, caption, and last log. It's a simple concept, but it's an effective simple concept with enough little flourishes to make it work. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This is something that actually made me think for a second. Nice job with this one.

+1

item.title: Re: 1152
item.content:encoded: He probably did get scratches resulting from it, I doubt the agent is as well groomed as he once was after being in containment as an animal for some time. They see the closest thing.

item.title: Re: 1152
item.content:encoded: I understand that, but what injuries did he sustain? Did they see it as scratches, even though it was actually a bruise? Or is it something else?

item.title: Re: 1152
item.content:encoded: Because raccoons have very sharp claws, they most likely saw the raccoon jumping onto the agent.

item.title: Re: 1152
item.content:encoded:
This should be tagged humanoid instead of animal, since tagging isn't in-universe. If this is a human, then it should be tagged likewise. Also:

11:02AM SCP-1152 attacks Agent █████

I'm guessing he punched the agent, so how did anyone not notice that?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This is two things.
It's something that will make you go "Huh, that took me a second."
And it's something one loves or hates.
+1

After several days of getting feedback until almost nothing was called out it is live.

spoilers

+ show block
– hide block

It is the agent, not a raccoon.

Yes the picture is viewed as a raccoon in universe too.
